author: @kilokilos
text: How is the efficiency here, it seems you have a horizontal and the windmill is a vertical? The blades are many and covers the whole area that might give a better performance?

author: @seeker1015
text: Robert, my mill has a pair of small gears on the fan shaft driving a larger pair of gears that have a connecting rod mounted on a pin in each gear. these pull up the pump rod end, while being held square by a parallel slot, the end has a roller mounted on it. I think there'd  be less friction with that arrangement than a Scotch yoke.

replies.author: @seeker1015
replies.text: Was having a look at the head today, the gears are cleverly situated so that on the up stroke, the connecting rods are moving vertically and on the downstroke, they go at an angle minimising wear on the roller and central vertical guide.

text: Yes! We are over-focused on efficiency when there is untapped resource everywhere

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: A swivel on a connecting rod from a simple crankshaft is easier to build and robust, but is difficult to maintain, near the top of the tower.   The U.S. farm style examples that  I've seen all suffer from terminal corrosion and lack of proper lubrication on all the bearing surfaces.  I've one in view outside my window, right now, that has not turned in years -- rusted solid.  A scotch yoke or excentric driven pump, using modern materials and sealed bearings, would be just the ticket.

author: @threeMetreJim
text: Efficiency is only bought up by people selling energy - it's got to be economically viable; turn a (healthy) profit. I've seen canal boaters get by on 2 solar panels and a battery bank for about 8 months of the year. Just the awful British weather for the other 4 months means the solar panels and batteries are not quite enough. Then there is the issue of heating / cooking - many burn logs, but that is a little polluting and you only have so many trees, or gas which is convenient but expensive, and also a finite resource. It's really hard to get away from completely having to pay for energy unfortunately.

author: @Guardian_Arias
text:
Love it, I'm sure the drive could be an oval or an elliptical shape to reduced material cost and make it easier to balance.

Speaking of material cost, what kind of infill do you use? I find Cubic Subdivision at 20%, more than adequate if I use 3 walls/perimeters with a layer width of 120% that of the nozzle. So 0.48mm for a 0.4mm nozzle.

Fun fact one can print a 1.2mm wide line in a single pass with a single 0.4mm nozzle. Sounds crazy, but die swell is a thing, and it's a thing that needs to be abused.

text:
Hi Rob, I thought I'd print your maglev turbine last night, I did the base, unfortunately the donut magnets I have from old microwave magnetrons are too small, your design appears to be for magnets 75mm O/D, I think you mentioned "speaker magnets", not sure where to source these from?
Also, I spent about 20 quid on n52 magnets to mount in the cage, that's probably going to be fine, although I have quite a few rectangular ferrite magnets I'd like to incorporate into the model instead, I'm thinking of knocking out a few of your maglev generators to mount on the hut roof, I'm sure there's an easy way to rework the design in Tinkercad, however I'm stuck, any chance of a Tinkercad masterclass video?

Ive found this quite inspiring.
What is a good mechanism for that style of windmill (one that changes direction with the wind)
To drive a generator on the ground?
Obviously a shaft might not be good as it would want to twist the mill itself
Edit: this isnt very articulate, but a problem i forsee with using a crownwheel and pinion from the mill to the floor is that it would want to torque the mill round instead of drive the shaft.

author: @iangregory3719
text:
One night in the pub I suggested that surplus renewable energy be used to split water into hydrogen, which can be easily stored for future use - fuel cells/generators - always the same response...."its way too inefficient,  you loose X amount in the process...etc.". My answer is "yeh...so...your initial energy input is absolutely free, and totally inexhaustible...so efficiency is not a major factor". I think the obsession with maximum efficiency is clouding the vision of a lot of people and organisations. 

 Really liking the new "Dr. Who"  look Rob 😂

author: @adamdecurtins3405
text: I live in the US and I've been somewhat regularly checking a couple for-sale sites in hopes of scoring about a 6 foot diameter wind wheel from one of those old pumps, and then turn it vertical and use it on a darwin style wind tower! The amount of torque those wheels produce even in a low wind is just crazy and it's because they have so many blades. That's the trade off. Less blades on the modern wind turbines gives you more efficiency in the system but having a whole but bunch of blades is what you need when torque will do!

author: @The-SaltiestPretzel
text: There's definitely something to be said about a fixation on only building energy gathering infrastructure when it as efficient and vast as possible. From a cost perspective, we of course want the most "bang for our buck", but this could be achieved with an emphasis on modularity and modifiability in design. A system that is low-cost, smaller in scope and less optimal than it could be is still producing more usable energy than a system that is very efficient, capable of providing for many but not yet built.

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
Very interesting video bringing back some memories!
I spent a few hours each time replacing the leather seals on my windpump - once even at 21:00 in the dark!
Fortunately my borehole was relatively shallow at about 95 m, so the effort of raising the pipes was not as great as in the case of others who had 300 m deep boreholes.
Later innovations replaced the steel pipes and rods with plastic to reduce wear and corrosion.

replies.author: @davidblyth5495
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkeringI concur - when one looks at the "ilities", its maintainability is quite high - few moving parts and the leather seal is easy to make if needed.
Most parts are easily manufactured too.

@davidblyth5495

I used to put the brake on the windmill when the winds were strong so I'd avoid damage.

author: @johnparsons9620
text:
Robert on the theme of simplicity & multiple redundancy, 
I have always wondered why pump storage  power stations don't also use wind pumps to supplement their main electrical turbo pumps.   
For every ton of water pumped by a wind pump from  the lower basin to the top basin, that is one ton less of water, required to be pumped using off peak electricity.  With multiple wind pumps, there is no single point of failure  and there is multiple redundancy using a simple technology.  On  wind free days the turbo pump has to do more work.

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text: The key problem is, as I understand it, control. In theory wind pumps capable of contributing useful quantities of water could continue pumping enough to overtop the damn and cause catastrophic failures. Electric pumps stop pumping when you turn them, or their breakers, off, meaning that they don't just run when no one is watching. They are also MUCH easier to control with electric safety measures than a direct mechanical pump. A simple float switch killing a relay can watch the water level while a mechanical clutch requires effort to design and integrate with safety measures. Certainly there are ways to make it work, but minimal rewards for potentially maximum costs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't know why not - Vidar says it is control - I disagree control could be implemented with release valves and float sensors - i suspect lack of imagination lol

replies.author: @johnparsons9620
replies.text: Canals & dams solved this problem with bypass channels, even simple syphons could be used.  Better still generate power for a few minutes.

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text: The problem is again, that if those bypass canals or syphons fail the water is pumped without any control, and you can end up with catastrophic failure, meaning that you are gambling millions of dollars and some number of lives to get a few thousand dollars in power a year... As a power company it just is not worth implementing and maintaining the added complexity. As an individual it would make sense because a few of these pumps could provide significant energy on an individual scale. So I should have said that it was control and scale, not just control.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ​@vidard9863 I am sorry mate and i don't mean to be rude but that's a silly thing to say as that is just a problem associated with dams not specifically to wind pumps per se - i get it you hate wind powered water pumps but what i get from what you are saying now is that you hate them because you hate them and that's it

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: Attach a bucket on a string to the horizontal axle or the pin of the yoke, let it wind up and you have a second storage of energy. you could let it unwind when there is no wind and you would still be pumping water, or spinning a generator.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: like the clutch video we did?

replies.author: @JehuMcSpooran
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yeah, that could be useful. I was thinking that if the pump operated as a pump and brought water up high before discharging it to what ever pond there is, it could act as a power smoothing device, like a metaphorical flywheel or capacitor, for allowing the pump to still operate if the wind dies down or varies. While the yoke, as you mentioned, converts rotational motion into linear motion, there is still that rotation of the horizontal shaft that you could use as a PTO (power take off) for other uses...depending on load and speed.

author: @DreadX10
text:
As a Dutchman, I don't think about those tall white poles when you mention a 'windmill'.

Your 3D-printed yoke seems nice but I have to wonder about the extra friction being generated when the pieces lose their initial alignment. If the geared wheel gets skewed a bit (so that it not only pushes up and down but also sideways/front-to-back), it will push the sliding part against the frame and the heat generated would cause more wear then you would want.
How many revolutions would you get before failure? Is it still in a practical range (10^5-10^7 revolutions)?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the print is to show the concept mate - if you worry about it build it from something else - plenty of options

replies.author: @JonLovestheLord
replies.text: Scotch yokes can be made a lot more efficient with a simple bearing around the drive pin that goes in the slot, the drag friction becomes rolling friction.

replies.author: @mrab4222
replies.text: You don't think of them as windmills because they aren't windmills. A windmill is a mill that's powered by wind, but it's not a mill. It's a wind turbine.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: @JonLovestheLord I should learn to always scroll through before posting

replies.author: @DreadX10
replies.text:
@JonLovestheLord Miss-alignment will cause a horizontal force on the yoke. Wasn't talking about the pin (as this is usually a roller-bearing).
Eliminating destructive forces in a design is always good (also for efficiency).

author: @Howzit73
text: Rob I enjoy these videos. The "wind turbine" you are using, are there any drawings for that please? I want to print my own and start playing around with power generation. I am from South Africa and we need decent alternative sources. Thank you and keep the videos coming.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not very good mate - i have it so i use it  -but you would be better off with savonius or a ugrinksy turbine

replies.author: @Howzit73
replies.text: Thank you very much I'll have a look into them.

author: @atntaltd
text:
exactly efficiency doesn't mean a thing unless CONTEXT applied
rob has drilled that into me now .....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - awesome mate

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text: That is the thing, efficiency MUST be a contextual concept. "Current" conception of efficiency is how well you can convert input energy into output energy. But that means that the efficiency is calculated against the energy input resource. Efficiency calculated against cost, materials, or complexity to power output all are important.

replies.author: @MIck1-10
replies.text: A point in favor of the old style windmill types is all the parts seem to be recyclable,a huge tick in the box.

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text: @MIck1-10 even better than recycle able they are repairable! While not mutually exclusive, repair means that many parts never even need to be made again, meaning less input "costs", while the parts that break are generally simple enough to be easily recycled, meaning that they are much more likely to be recycled.

replies.author: @MIck1-10
replies.text: @vidard9863 very true.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: There also existed ai steam based refrigerator.  The directed stream of steam was configured to create low pressure to lower the boiling point of enclosed body of water held within same enclosure as the directed stream of steam  above the body of water. We've all noticed how cold it feels to spill alcohol or acetone or ether or even  petrol on our hands .
